result:
**The Peculiar Art of Noticing**

I've been thinking lately about attention—not the kind we seek from others, but the kind we pay to the world around us. There's something both tragic and beautiful about how much of life passes by unnoticed, like water through cupped hands, while we're busy looking at our phones or worrying about tomorrow's meeting or trying to remember if we locked the front door.

Yesterday, I watched a woman at a bus stop carefully folding a gum wrapper into smaller and smaller squares until it became impossibly tiny, a little silver nugget she then slipped into her pocket. Why? I'll never know. But for those few seconds, that small act of methodical folding seemed to contain an entire universe of human peculiarity. It made me wonder how many such moments I miss every day, how many tiny dramas and private rituals unfold in my peripheral vision while I'm absorbed in my own thoughts.

We live in an age that celebrates productivity and efficiency, where every moment should be optimized, every gap in the schedule filled with something useful. But what if the most useful thing we could do is simply notice? Not document, not photograph, not share—just notice.

There's a particular quality of light that happens in late afternoon, especially in autumn, when the sun angles low and everything becomes outlined in gold. Photographers call it the golden hour, but that seems too grand a name for something so quietly miraculous. It's the kind of light that makes even mundane things—a chain-link fence, a pile of leaves, the side of a building—seem temporarily blessed. I think about how this same light has been falling at this same angle for billions of years, long before there were eyes to see it, and how it will continue long after we're gone. There's comfort in that continuity.

The Japanese have a phrase, "mono no aware," which roughly translates to "the pathos of things" or "a gentle sadness of transience." It's the bittersweet awareness that all things are temporary, that beauty is fleeting, that every moment is already passing even as we experience it. This might sound melancholic, but I find it oddly liberating. If everything is temporary, then perhaps we can release our white-knuckled grip on trying to control and preserve everything. Perhaps we can just be present instead.

I remember reading once that the average person will spend about five years of their life waiting in lines. Five years! At first, this statistic depressed me—what a waste, I thought. But then I reconsidered. What if those five years aren't lost time but found time? Time when we're forced to slow down, to stand still, to exist without agenda. Time when we might notice the pattern of tiles on the floor, the rhythm of other people's breathing, the way a child three people ahead is trying to balance on one foot, arms outstretched like wings.

The truth is, I think we're afraid of really noticing things. Because to truly pay attention is to acknowledge the overwhelming complexity and beauty and sadness of existence. It's easier to skim the surface, to stay busy, to keep our observations shallow and our connections brief. But when we do stop and really look—really see—we might catch a glimpse of something profound in the ordinary.

Consider trees, for instance. We pass them every day, these silent witnesses to our hurried lives. But a tree, if you really look at it, is an absurd miracle. It's essentially a giant pump system that defies gravity, pulling water up from the ground sometimes hundreds of feet into the air, converting sunlight into sugar, exhaling oxygen as a waste product that happens to keep us alive. Each leaf is a tiny solar panel, each root hair a drinking straw. Trees communicate with each other through underground fungal networks, sharing nutrients and warnings about insect attacks. They can live for thousands of years, outlasting empires and ideologies. And yet we walk past them as if they're just street furniture.

This kind of deep noticing doesn't require any special equipment or training. Children do it naturally—watch a four-year-old examine a bug or a puddle and you'll see the kind of absorbed attention that monks spend years trying to achieve. Somewhere along the way to adulthood, most of us lose this capacity for wonder. We trade it for practical knowledge, for the ability to categorize and dismiss: that's just a pigeon, that's just a sunset, that's just rain.

But "just" is the enemy of wonder. There's no such thing as "just" anything if you look closely enough. A pigeon is a descendant of dinosaurs with magnets in its brain that help it navigate using Earth's magnetic field. A sunset is the scattering of photons that have traveled 93 million miles from a nuclear furnace in space. Rain is the planet's circulatory system, the same water that dinosaurs drank, falling from the sky.

Perhaps what I'm really advocating for is a kind of reverent inefficiency, a deliberate slowness that allows for surprise. In a world that increasingly demands our attention be monetized, distracted, and fragmented, the simple act of sustained, agenda-free observation becomes almost revolutionary. It's a quiet form of resistance against the tyranny of notifications and the endless scroll.

So tomorrow, maybe I'll take the long way home. Maybe I'll sit on a bench and watch people walk their dogs. Maybe I'll notice the way shadows change shape as clouds pass overhead, or how everyone has a slightly different way of opening a door. These aren't productive activities. They won't improve my career or optimize my life. But they might remind me that I'm alive, here, now, in this strange and temporary moment, paying attention to the beautiful, heartbreaking, ordinary miracle of it all.
